Title: Elucidation of the final steps in Taxol biosynthesis and its biotechnological production

Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: M24 promoter of Mirabilis mosaic virus (high-level constitutive transient expression in tobacco); galactose-inducible yeast expression from episomal vectors for yeast reconstruction
Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: N. benthamiana not specified; yeast strain EGY48
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: PAM can be used upstream to generate the non-proteinogenic precursor β-phenylalanine in heterologous Taxol platforms, reducing the need for external β-phenylalanine feeding. It is suitable for pathway stacking with CoA ligase and BAPT modules in plant or microbial chassis. Ensure sufficient downstream flux because PAM alone is not rate-limiting once β-phenylalanine supply is adequate.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enables conversion of α-phenylalanine to β-phenylalanine, supplying the side-chain precursor required for Taxol biosynthesis.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transient expression in N. benthamiana followed by UPLC-qTOF-MS detection of β-phenylalanine; pathway reconstruction with downstream product formation.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Agroinfiltration in tobacco; exogenous baccatin III or 10-deacetyl-baccatin III feeding; yeast cultures grown at 20°C for improved pathway function; β-phenylalanine feeding in yeast when PAM not deployed there.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Heterologous transient expression in tobacco leaves; not an endogenous applied-species gene.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Not specifically reported here; previously known Taxus pathway-related gene.
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq/co-expression analysis across Taxus chinensis var. mairei, Taxus yunnanensis and Taxus wallichiana transcriptomes (59 datasets total); no accession numbers specified in the provided text
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: PAM activity was sufficient in tobacco such that exogenous β-phenylalanine did not increase N-debenzoyl-2′-deoxy-taxol production, indicating precursor supply from PAM was adequate in that system.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No direct tradeoff reported; in engineered systems increased precursor diversion from phenylalanine may potentially affect aromatic amino acid metabolism.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PAM function was experimentally confirmed in tobacco by β-phenylalanine detection. 2. PAM supplied sufficient β-phenylalanine to drive efficient N-debenzoyl-2′-deoxy-taxol formation with downstream enzymes. 3. It serves as an effective upstream precursor-supplying module for late Taxol pathway reconstruction.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Taxus bark and needles; in this study produced heterologously in tobacco leaves and yeast cultures
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antimitotic anticancer compound used clinically against multiple cancers

Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: M24 promoter of Mirabilis mosaic virus in tobacco; galactose-inducible yeast expression from episomal vectors
Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: N. benthamiana not specified; yeast strain EGY48
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: DBAT can be used as a flexible acetylation module to exploit a metabolic grid between 10-deacetyl and acetylated taxoids. Useful for route balancing when using 10-deacetyl-baccatin III-rich feedstocks. Should be stacked with BAPT, T2′OGD and T3′NBT depending on substrate entry point.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Acetylates 10-deacetyl-baccatin III to baccatin III and also acetylates 10-deacetyl-N-debenzoyl-2′-deoxy-taxol and 10-deacetyl-N-debenzoyl-taxol to their acetylated counterparts, supporting a late-pathway metabolic grid.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Tobacco feeding experiments with chemically synthesized intermediates and UPLC-qTOF-MS metabolite profiling; literature-supported known activity.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Exogenous feeding of 10-deacetyl-baccatin III, 10-deacetyl-N-debenzoyl-2′-deoxy-taxol, or 10-deacetyl-N-debenzoyl-taxol to agroinfiltrated tobacco leaves.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Heterologous transient expression in tobacco leaves.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Grouped in one Taxol-gene co-expression cluster together with taxadiene synthase, T5αH, T10βH, TAT, TBT, DBAT and BAPT.
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq/co-expression analysis across 59 Taxus transcriptomes; no accession numbers specified in provided text
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: DBAT is promiscuous and helps demonstrate that late Taxol biosynthesis is a metabolic grid rather than a strict linear sequence from baccatin III only.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Promiscuity may generate multiple acetylated intermediates, complicating flux analysis in engineered hosts.
Common_Genes.Source_Species: Taxus species (exact source not specified here for experiments; previously characterized Taxus enzyme)
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DBAT acetylates not only 10-deacetyl-baccatin III but also 10-deacetyl late intermediates 6 and 7. 2. This promiscuity supports a metabolic grid in the late Taxol pathway. 3. DBAT therefore expands possible entry points for bioconversion routes.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Taxus bark and needles; heterologous tobacco and yeast systems in this study

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: M24 promoter of Mirabilis mosaic virus in tobacco; galactose-inducible episomal yeast expression
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: N. benthamiana not specified; yeast strain EGY48
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: T2′OGD is the correct enzyme for the final side-chain C2′α hydroxylation and should replace previously proposed TB506 in any Taxol engineering platform. It is a high-value stacking target with BAPT and T3′NBT for complete late-pathway reconstruction. Suitable for microbial bioconversion from baccatin III or 10-deacetyl-baccatin III. Key caution: acts before 3′-N benzoylation and does not accept 2′-deoxy-taxol, so pathway order must be preserved.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates the C2′ position of the β-phenylalanyl side chain of N-debenzoyl-2′-deoxy-taxol to form N-debenzoyl-taxol; also hydroxylates 10-deacetyl-N-debenzoyl-2′-deoxy-taxol to 10-deacetyl-N-debenzoyl-taxol.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Strongly promotes production of N-debenzoyl-taxol from N-debenzoyl-2′-deoxy-taxol and enables downstream Taxol formation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene co-expression-guided candidate discovery; transient expression in tobacco with pathway reconstruction; feeding of synthetic substrate; comparison to authentic standards by UPLC-qTOF-MS; reconstruction in yeast with intermediate conversion measurements.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor of Taxol and is required before final benzoylation
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Agroinfiltration in tobacco leaves; exogenous N-debenzoyl-2′-deoxy-taxol, baccatin III, or 10-deacetyl-baccatin III feeding; methyl-jasmonate included among conditions used in co-expression analysis; yeast cultivation at 20°C.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Heterologous transient expression in tobacco leaves and episomal expression in yeast.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Identified among T. chinensis OGD genes whose expression strongly correlates with known Taxol biosynthetic gene clusters across Taxus tissues and conditions.
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Downstream, penultimate step before final benzoylation
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Comparative RNA-seq/co-expression analysis using 59 transcriptome datasets from Taxus chinensis var. mairei, T. yunnanensis and T. wallichiana; no public accession numbers specified in provided text
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: TB506, the previously proposed CYP, failed direct validation. T2′OGD nearly completely converted available substrate in tobacco and consumed 71 ± 1% of available N-debenzoyl-2′-deoxy-taxol in yeast. It also acts on 10-deacetyl substrate, supporting a pathway grid. It does not hydroxylate 2′-deoxy-taxol, proving hydroxylation precedes benzoylation.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Late side-chain tailoring steps of Taxol biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No direct fitness penalty reported; none of the later steps including T2′OGD appeared bottlenecking at current engineering stage.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key required penultimate step; not identified as current bottleneck in yeast
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Variety: Likely var. mairei cDNA reference used for synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. T2′OGD, not TB506, catalyses the missing C2′α hydroxylation step in late Taxol biosynthesis. 2. It efficiently converts N-debenzoyl-2′-deoxy-taxol to N-debenzoyl-taxol in tobacco and yeast. 3. Its substrate specificity establishes that hydroxylation precedes 3′-N benzoylation. 4. It also accepts 10-deacetyl intermediates, supporting a metabolic grid.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Taxus bark and needles; heterologous tobacco leaves and yeast cultures

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: M24 promoter of Mirabilis mosaic virus in tobacco; galactose-inducible episomal yeast expression
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: N. benthamiana not specified; yeast strain EGY48
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: T3′NBT is the preferred terminal benzoyltransferase for Taxol engineering and should replace DBTNBT for accurate pathway reconstruction. It is ideal for stacking with T2′OGD and benzoyl-CoA-supplying modules. Requires benzoyl-CoA availability, making co-engineering of benzoyl-CoA supply important.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers a benzoyl group from benzoyl-CoA to the 3′-N position of N-debenzoyl-taxol to form Taxol; also benzoylates 10-deacetyl-N-debenzoyl-taxol to form 10-deacetyl-taxol.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Directly forms Taxol from N-debenzoyl-taxol with high efficiency and strongly outperforms the previously proposed DBTNBT.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene co-expression-guided candidate identification; transient expression in tobacco with authentic standard comparison by UPLC-qTOF-MS; yeast reconstruction; quantification of Taxol accumulation; substrate specificity assays.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target product Taxol in the terminal step
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Agroinfiltration in tobacco leaves; exogenous baccatin III or 10-deacetyl-baccatin III feeding; benzoyl-CoA supply enhanced in yeast by a heterologous module; yeast grown at 20°C.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Heterologous transient expression in tobacco leaves and episomal expression in yeast.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Identified from BAHD-family candidates showing co-expression with Taxol pathway genes in Taxus datasets.
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: T2′OGD; BAPT; CoAL(A312G); benzoyl-CoA supply enzymes PAL; CNL; CDH; KAT1
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Comparative RNA-seq/co-expression analysis using 59 transcriptome datasets from three Taxus species; no accession numbers specified in provided text
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: T3′NBT converted ~75% of available N-debenzoyl-taxol to Taxol in tobacco and gave 280-fold higher Taxol than background/endogenous tobacco activity or DBTNBT. It strictly requires prior C2′α hydroxylation and does not accept N-debenzoyl-2′-deoxy-taxol or 10-deacetyl-N-debenzoyl-2′-deoxy-taxol.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Late side-chain terminal decoration in Taxol biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No direct growth penalty reported; terminal-step efficiency can become limited by benzoyl-CoA supply, necessitating precursor-pathway engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Terminal determinant; not limiting once benzoyl-CoA is sufficient
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Variety: Likely var. mairei cDNA reference used for synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. T3′NBT is the correct terminal 3′-N benzoyltransferase in Taxol biosynthesis. 2. It efficiently converts N-debenzoyl-taxol to Taxol in tobacco and yeast, unlike DBTNBT. 3. It requires prior C2′α hydroxylation, thereby fixing pathway order. 4. Its performance improves further when benzoyl-CoA supply is engineered.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Taxus bark and needles; heterologous tobacco leaves and yeast cultures

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: M24 promoter of Mirabilis mosaic virus in tobacco; galactose-inducible episomal yeast expression
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: N. benthamiana not specified; yeast strain EGY48
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: BAPT is a core side-chain transferase and a major engineering target because it is a principal bottleneck in yeast. It should be optimized by homolog screening, protein engineering, fusion tags, or host selection. Essential for conversion of baccatin III or 10-deacetyl-baccatin III to C13 side-chain-containing intermediates.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers the β-phenylalanoyl side chain from β-phenylalanoyl-CoA to the C13 hydroxyl of baccatin III to form N-debenzoyl-2′-deoxy-taxol; also acts on 10-deacetyl-baccatin III to form 10-deacetyl-N-debenzoyl-2′-deoxy-taxol.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enables C13 side-chain addition, creating the immediate substrate for T2′OGD and thereby committing flux into late Taxol biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Tobacco co-expression assays with CoA ligases and metabolite profiling; yeast pathway reconstruction; protein engineering (BAPTm, MBP fusion) linked to product titer changes.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor of downstream Taxol intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Agroinfiltration in tobacco; exogenous baccatin III or 10-deacetyl-baccatin III feeding; yeast temperature reduction from 30°C to 20°C improved BAPT protein levels and activity.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Heterologous transient expression in tobacco leaves and episomal yeast expression.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Part of one Taxol-gene co-expression cluster together with taxadiene synthase, T5αH, T10βH, TAT19, TBT, DBAT and others.
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: Not reported for BAPT in this paper
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: PAM; CoAL(A312G); TBPCCL; AAE16; T2′OGD; T3′NBT; MBP in engineered fusion variant
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Downstream side-chain installation step before C2′ hydroxylation
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq/co-expression analysis across 59 Taxus transcriptomes; no accession numbers specified in provided text
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Taxus cuspidata BAPT was used. CoAL(A312G) supported much more efficient product formation than TBPCCL or AAE16. BAPT was low-abundance/poorly performing in yeast; engineered variants BAPTm and MBPig3BAPTm increased N-debenzoyl-2′-deoxy-taxol production by 27% and 77%, respectively, relative to wild-type BAPT.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Major bottleneck in yeast due to stability/solubility limitations; poor performance restricts overall Taxol yield.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Major bottleneck in current yeast system
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BAPT catalyses C13 side-chain addition to baccatin III and 10-deacetyl-baccatin III. 2. It is necessary for formation of N-debenzoyl-2′-deoxy-taxol, the substrate for T2′OGD. 3. In yeast, BAPT performance is poor and constitutes a major bottleneck. 4. Protein engineering and MBP fusion improve BAPT productivity.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Taxus bark and needles; heterologous tobacco leaves and yeast cultures

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: M24 promoter of Mirabilis mosaic virus in tobacco; galactose-inducible episomal yeast expression
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: N. benthamiana not specified; yeast strain EGY48
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: β-phenylalanoyl-CoA supply for late Taxol biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: CoAL(A312G) is an effective surrogate β-phenylalanine CoA ligase for heterologous Taxol reconstruction, especially where native Taxus ligases are inefficient. It should be used provisionally until a better native Taxus enzyme is identified. Pair with BAPT for C13 side-chain assembly.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Activates β-phenylalanine to β-phenylalanoyl-CoA, supplying the acyl donor for BAPT-mediated transfer to baccatin III.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Strongly enhances formation of N-debenzoyl-2′-deoxy-taxol compared with native candidate CoA ligases TBPCCL and AAE16.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Tobacco co-expression with BAPT and PAM; yeast reconstruction and comparative product titers; metabolite profiling by UPLC-qTOF-MS.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Agroinfiltration in tobacco; exogenous baccatin III feeding; yeast culture at 20°C.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Heterologous expression in tobacco and codon-optimized expression in yeast.
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Upstream precursor activation for late side-chain assembly
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: No omics specific to this gene reported
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Derived from Penicillium chrysogenum. Used because proposed Taxus ligases TBPCCL and AAE16 were weak in tobacco and yeast. Authors note a newly reported candidate Taxus CoA ligase should be tested in future.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May still be suboptimal in yeast despite good performance in tobacco; non-native surrogate may not support industrially robust flux.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supports a potentially limiting precursor-supply step; current surrogate not fully optimized in yeast
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CoAL(A312G) robustly supports β-phenylalanoyl-CoA-dependent side-chain assembly in tobacco. 2. It outperforms the proposed native ligases TBPCCL and AAE16 in both tobacco and yeast. 3. It enabled late-pathway reconstruction to Taxol, but may still be insufficient for optimal yeast flux.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Taxus bark and needles; heterologous tobacco leaves and yeast cultures

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: M24 promoter of Mirabilis mosaic virus in tobacco
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: β-phenylalanoyl-CoA supply for late Taxol biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: TBPCCL is a native candidate CoA ligase but underperformed in both tobacco and yeast compared with CoAL(A312G), so it is currently not recommended as the primary engineering choice without further optimization.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Proposed to activate β-phenylalanine to β-phenylalanoyl-CoA for BAPT-mediated side-chain transfer.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports only low formation of N-debenzoyl-2′-deoxy-taxol in pathway reconstruction.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Co-expression with BAPT in tobacco and comparative reconstruction in yeast followed by metabolite profiling.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Agroinfiltration; exogenous baccatin III feeding; yeast reconstruction comparisons.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Initially reported from T. baccata; clearly less efficient than CoAL(A312G) in this study.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TBPCCL can support some N-debenzoyl-2′-deoxy-taxol formation with BAPT. 2. It is much less efficient than CoAL(A312G) in tobacco and yeast. 3. It is unlikely to be the best enzyme for engineering the late Taxol pathway.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: M24 promoter of Mirabilis mosaic virus in tobacco
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: β-phenylalanoyl-CoA supply for late Taxol biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: AAE16 is another native candidate CoA ligase, but current evidence indicates low utility for engineering compared with CoAL(A312G). It may require re-evaluation only if optimized variants or contexts emerge.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Proposed to activate β-phenylalanine to β-phenylalanoyl-CoA for side-chain assembly.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports only low levels of N-debenzoyl-2′-deoxy-taxol formation when paired with BAPT.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Tobacco co-expression assays and comparative yeast reconstruction with metabolite profiling.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Agroinfiltration; exogenous baccatin III feeding; yeast reconstruction comparisons.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Previously proposed from Taxus chinensis; markedly less efficient than CoAL(A312G).
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AAE16 is capable of supporting some late-pathway flux with BAPT. 2. It is substantially less efficient than CoAL(A312G) in both tobacco and yeast. 3. It is not the preferred engineering enzyme for this step.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: M24 promoter of Mirabilis mosaic virus in tobacco
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Late Taxol biosynthesis (previously proposed terminal step)
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: DBTNBT should not be used as the main terminal benzoyltransferase for Taxol reconstruction because it poorly benzoylates the native substrate N-debenzoyl-taxol. Its activity on surrogate substrate 2′-deoxy intermediates may still be useful analytically, but it is unsuitable for accurate production platforms.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Benzoylates N-debenzoyl-2′-deoxy-taxol to 2′-deoxy-taxol, but does not efficiently convert native substrate N-debenzoyl-taxol to Taxol under tested conditions.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Fails to raise Taxol above endogenous tobacco background from N-debenzoyl-taxol; therefore not the correct efficient terminal Taxol synthase.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transient expression in tobacco producing N-debenzoyl-taxol; authentic standard comparison by UPLC-qTOF-MS; side assay on surrogate substrate N-debenzoyl-2′-deoxy-taxol.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Competes as misassigned surrogate-acting branch rather than authentic terminal step
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Agroinfiltration in tobacco with exogenous baccatin III feeding and pathway co-expression.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Heterologous transient expression in tobacco leaves.
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Previously proposed terminal step; reassigned as non-native-substrate-active enzyme
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Taxol detected without DBTNBT was attributed to endogenous tobacco activity. DBTNBT remained active on surrogate substrate, explaining historical misassignment.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Misuse in engineering leads to incorrect pathway reconstruction and low Taxol yield.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not the authentic terminal control step
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DBTNBT is active on the surrogate substrate N-debenzoyl-2′-deoxy-taxol. 2. It does not efficiently catalyse Taxol formation from the native substrate N-debenzoyl-taxol in tobacco. 3. Previous assignment of DBTNBT as the terminal Taxol benzoyltransferase was likely confounded by endogenous host activity.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: M24 promoter of Mirabilis mosaic virus in tobacco
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Previously proposed C2′ hydroxylation step in Taxol biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: TB506 should not be used as the C2′ hydroxylase in Taxol engineering because direct assays failed to show conversion of N-debenzoyl-2′-deoxy-taxol to N-debenzoyl-taxol. Its earlier assignment should be considered incorrect or at least unsupported.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Previously proposed to hydroxylate N-debenzoyl-2′-deoxy-taxol at C2′, but no direct conversion was observed in this study.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: No detectable production of N-debenzoyl-taxol from the native substrate in tobacco; does not support the missing pathway step.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transient expression in tobacco, western blot confirmation of protein production, feeding/combined pathway assays, UPLC-qTOF-MS comparison to authentic standard.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Previously proposed immediate-precursor-forming step, but not validated
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Agroinfiltration and exogenous feeding of N-debenzoyl-2′-deoxy-taxol in tobacco.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Protein was expressed, but no direct product formation was seen, strongly arguing against the prior functional assignment based on indirect downstream detection in other systems.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Using TB506 in engineering would waste flux and misassign pathway logic.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TB506 protein accumulates in tobacco but does not produce N-debenzoyl-taxol from N-debenzoyl-2′-deoxy-taxol. 2. Previous assignment of TB506 as the C2′ hydroxylase is unsupported by direct evidence. 3. T2′OGD, not TB506, catalyses this step.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Chromosomal integration in yeast cytosol-directed module
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Benzoyl-CoA supply for Taxol terminal benzoylation
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: PAL2 is useful to build a benzoyl-CoA supply module in microbial Taxol production by converting phenylalanine to cinnamic acid. Best deployed together with CNL, CDH and KAT1, especially when T3′NBT becomes limited by benzoyl-CoA supply.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Deaminates phenylalanine to cinnamic acid, providing the entry substrate for the heterologous benzoyl-CoA pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to increased benzoyl-CoA supply and supports a 1.6-fold increase in Taxol titer when combined with CNL, CDH and KAT1.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Chromosomal integration in yeast as part of a benzoyl-CoA module and measurement of Taxol titer improvement.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Yeast cultures supplied with baccatin III and β-phenylalanine; galactose induction at 20°C.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Chromosomally integrated heterologous expression in yeast cytosol-directed pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Upstream precursor supply to benzoyl-CoA module
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Peroxisomal targeting context pertains to Petunia enzymes; PAL from Arabidopsis supplied cinnamic acid to the module.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Possible diversion of phenylalanine from host metabolism, though no growth penalty was reported.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supportive precursor-supply step; not individually tested as bottleneck
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PAL was incorporated to feed cinnamic acid into a heterologous benzoyl-CoA module. 2. Together with downstream benzoyl-CoA enzymes it increased Taxol production in yeast. 3. It supports terminal benzoylation by improving benzoyl-CoA availability.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Chromosomal integration in yeast cytosol-directed module
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Benzoyl-CoA supply for Taxol terminal benzoylation
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: CNL is a useful component of heterologous benzoyl-CoA supply modules supporting Taxol production. It should be combined with PAL, CDH and KAT1 and expressed without peroxisomal targeting for cytosolic coupling to T3′NBT.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Ligates CoA to cinnamic acid to form cinnamoyl-CoA.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports enhanced benzoyl-CoA production and higher Taxol titer when part of the introduced module.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Module integration in yeast and Taxol titer increase in combination with other benzoyl-CoA pathway genes.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Yeast cultivation at 20°C with exogenous baccatin III and β-phenylalanine.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Chromosomally integrated heterologous expression in engineered yeast.
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: First CoA-activation step in benzoyl-CoA module
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Peroxisomal targeting signal removed for cytosolic function in yeast.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supportive supply step; not individually resolved
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Cytosolic in engineered yeast after removal of peroxisomal targeting signal
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CNL was used to activate cinnamic acid in the engineered benzoyl-CoA supply pathway. 2. The module increased Taxol titer in yeast. 3. Cytosolic retargeting enabled coupling to T3′NBT-mediated terminal benzoylation.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Chromosomal integration in yeast cytosol-directed module
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Benzoyl-CoA supply for Taxol terminal benzoylation
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: CDH is an essential mid-module enzyme for microbial benzoyl-CoA supply and should be coexpressed with PAL, CNL and KAT1 when boosting T3′NBT-dependent Taxol synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Bifunctionally hydrates cinnamoyl-CoA to 3-hydroxy-3-phenylpropanoyl-CoA and dehydrogenates it to 3-oxo-3-phenylpropanoyl-CoA.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports increased benzoyl-CoA availability and improved Taxol production in the engineered yeast module.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Chromosomal integration in yeast as part of a multi-enzyme module and associated Taxol titer improvement.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Yeast cultures at 20°C with exogenous baccatin III and β-phenylalanine.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Chromosomally integrated heterologous expression in engineered yeast.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Peroxisomal targeting signal removed for cytosolic function in yeast. Figure text has one typo as CHD, but enzyme described in text is CDH.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supportive midstream step; not individually resolved
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Cytosolic in engineered yeast after removal of peroxisomal targeting signal
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CDH was installed as the central bifunctional enzyme of the benzoyl-CoA module. 2. Together with PAL, CNL and KAT1 it improved Taxol synthesis. 3. Cytosolic deployment couples benzenoid precursor supply to terminal Taxol benzoylation.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Chromosomal integration in yeast cytosol-directed module
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Benzoyl-CoA supply for Taxol terminal benzoylation
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: KAT1 completes heterologous benzoyl-CoA generation and is important for supporting high T3′NBT flux. It should be coexpressed with PAL, CNL and CDH in cytosolic modules.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to increased benzoyl-CoA pool and improved Taxol titer in engineered yeast.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Chromosomal integration as part of the benzoyl-CoA module and observation of improved Taxol production.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies immediate co-substrate pool for terminal Taxol formation
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Yeast cultures at 20°C with exogenous baccatin III and β-phenylalanine.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Chromosomally integrated heterologous expression in engineered yeast.
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Terminal step of benzoyl-CoA supply module
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Peroxisomal targeting signal removed for cytosolic function in yeast.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supportive terminal supply step; not individually resolved
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Cytosolic in engineered yeast after removal of peroxisomal targeting signal
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. KAT1 completes the engineered benzoyl-CoA pathway by producing benzoyl-CoA. 2. The module increased Taxol titers by supporting T3′NBT. 3. Cytosolic retargeting was used to facilitate coupling to Taxol biosynthesis.
